data.title: Argan's Earnings Report Wasn't Luck, It Was A Sign
data.description:
As electricity demand has risen due to AI infrastructure, data centers and aging grids are needing to be upgraded or replaced, Argan (AGX) keeps finding itself in the exact right place at the right time. And while Argan isn’t a big flashy mega-cap technology stock, it is tied to those stocks' greatest need: Power.

Between their incredible quarterly report that they just released on March 26th and the subsequent gap up the next day, this is a good time to Own or Buy into Argan! And I’ll tell you why.

Argan, headquartered in Arlington, VA, is a power-construction and EPC company that uses its subsidiaries (like Gemma Power Systems) to a wide number of services that help to build out power assets. Assets such as engineering, procurement, construction, commissioning, operations, maintenance, project development, and even technical consulting services.

Now, I know that doesn’t exactly sound interesting, but it’s necessary and Argan knows how to sell it.

Argan has stayed disciplined, selective, and highly execution-focused with their projects. After looking at their revenue, margins, backlog and balance sheet, it's quite clear to me that Argan isn’t just chasing after growth. They’re focused on winning large projects and completing them the right way.

Argan also looks to be in its early stages, they have no debt and have been operating in a very narrow niche that is now becoming a major demand. Everyone knows that these AI data centers need massive amounts of power. The grid is being increasingly strained, and older assets need to be upgraded or replaced.

Argan even mentioned in one of their own reports that the growing demand from AI and data centers is putting more pressure on power systems. And we can see it! Companies are tripping over themselves in a race to solve these shortages. But Argan has shown us in their latest report that they have something special. Execution!

Argan’s CEO, David Watson, said that the company remains just as disciplined today as it was in its first year when it comes to pursuing opportunities and delivering successful projects.

This is backed up by Argan’s filings, which repeatedly emphasize things like accountability and operational focus, as well as strategic selectivity and as I pointed out earlier, disciplined execution.

That discipline speaks volumes, and it’s a big reason Argan is doing so well. They aren’t recklessly trying to win every bid, they're winning the right bids and putting all their effort into completing them well.

That’s some great leadership, I’d say. Not to mention, that their filings explicitly pointed out that some other industry participants have stepped back from certain opportunities in the power-construction niche. But while they’ve been backing off, Argan has remained focused and continued to operate with discipline instead of retreating!

That incredible foresight and execution tells me all I need to know about Argan’s leadership.

But what about the numbers?

Well, Argan’s ten-year average total return is 35.73% per year (vs. the S&P 500’s 21.3%), their five-year average total return is 65.29% per year (vs. the S&P 500’s 12.1%), their three-year average total return is an incredible 146.35% (vs. the S&P 500’s 20.2%).

Their twelve-month total return is a massive 377.8% (vs the S&P 500’s 11.5%), their three-month total return is 74.5% (vs. the S&P 500’s -8.1%), and their one-month total return is 25.2% (vs. the S&P 500’s -7.8%).

When I compare Argan’s short-term, intermediate term, and long-term performance against the other 5,300 stocks, ETFs, and Mutual Funds in my database, it gets a performance grade of A+. What about that alpha! Those are some impressive numbers! But we’re not done yet.

In their latest quarterly report, Argan reported very strong numbers. Their fourth-quarter revenue rose to $262.1 million, which is up 13.0% year over year.

Their gross profit increased to $65.6 million, which represents 25.0% of revenue, versus 20.5% in the prior-year quarter. Net income rose to $49.2 million, or $3.47 per diluted share, which is up from $31.4 million, or $2.22 per diluted share. EBITDA increased to $56.0 million from $39.3 million.

And while you can clearly see in the screenshot above, Argan’s full-year results were nothing to laugh at. They had a revenue of $944.6 million and net income of $137.8 million, or $9.74 per diluted share. Which is just absolutely terrific.

What’s even more impressive is that while in many project-based businesses numbers tend to only look good until execution falls apart, Argan’s leadership tied part of their margin improvement to their strong execution directly. Stating that a major reason was the early achievement of substantial completion at the Trumbull Energy Center.

Argan ended the fiscal year with approximately $895 million in cash, cash equivalents, and investments, which was up from $525 million a year earlier. They also had no debt! This is a huge positive for an infrastructure contractor.

It means Argan has financial flexibility, a bit of extra cushion against volatility, and if they choose to pursue opportunities, they can do so without depending on leverage.

Argan’s leadership really knows what it’s doing, while many other companies grow by taking on more balance-sheet risk, Argan has done the opposite. And it’s paid off!

All of this bodes quite well for the future of Argan, but you know what bodes even better? Backlog.

By the end of the last fiscal year, Argan’s backlog had already surged to about $2.9 billion, which is more than double what it was the year before ($1.4 billion). In the Power segment alone, backlog rose to more than $2.7 billion from about $1.3 billion. That’s some remarkable growth, and it’s the sort of growth that really helps a construction and infrastructure name stand out in its industry.

And it tells me that all of these recent earnings aren’t just because of one lucky quarter. Argan has a pipeline behind it, and if the past is any indication, quite the future ahead of it.

Now let’s look at a five-year valuation and target price on the shares. Performance looks backward while valuation looks forward.

In my valuation, I am using a next-year EPS estimate of $14.13. This is the current consensus estimate on the street. While this may seem lofty, keep in mind that Argan nearly doubled its backlog, and I assume a large portion of that backlog will convert to revenue and that margins will remain elevated as project execution stays strong.

Argan’s forward PE ratio comes in at 37.82X. The consensus estimated five-year annual growth rate is currently 15%. With the strong demand that Argan said there is for electricity, I assume the company can grow at that rate, especially thanks to its project pipeline.

The PEG ratio is 2.52X and the five-year target price is $998.73, with a five- year total upside potential of 85.58%. That’s a valuation grade of B+ in my grading system.

Now let's look at the risks.

One of the greatest risks that I see with Argan is that it remains far too heavily exposed to natural-gas-fired generation. Their annual report makes it quite clear that higher or more volatile natural gas prices could reduce project demand. As it would mean that developers may become a bit less willing to commit to new gas-fired capacity.

The second risk to me is competition. Argan isn't trying to become the biggest contractor in America, but it still has to compete with other firms, like MasTec, AECOM, Sterling Infrastructure and more.

However, what Argan has isn’t something easily replaced. Specialization, discipline and incredible project execution. So while they aren’t trying to be the best overall, they are aiming to become the best at building the kind of generation assets that are in the greatest demand right now.

Another risk is project execution. While Argan has shown discipline and execution, the business is one where delays, labor issues, and issues with procurement can very quickly hurt results if things get out of control. Now, Argan has been executing well up until now, and their philosophy makes me think that this isn’t too big of a worry, but EPC isn’t a risk-free business, and this is a real risk.

However, despite these risks, I think Argan is in a great position for a structural demand cycle. Electricity consumption is only going to rise, and more and more grids are going to need to be replaced.

If there was a time for dispatchable power to be on the boom, it’d be now. And among the companies looking to be a part of that boom, Argan certainly isn't the loudest, but after this last week, I would certainly say it’s becoming one of the more compelling ones.

After I assess the performance record of the stock and the current valuation, I examine a one-year chart of the stock. Is it in a #1 sideways trend, a #2 uptrend, a #3 consolidation trend, or a #4 downtrend? As you can see from the chart below, Argan is in a solid #2 uptrend. Furthermore, its recent earnings report has pushed the stock to all-time highs.

That’s why it remains a Strong Buy. We have owned the stock since 3/10/26 at a buy price of $477.32

Argan (AGX) is currently ranked 17 in my Best Stocks Now app database, with an overall Stock Grade of A.

data.title: IDRV: Taking This Self-Driving And EV ETF For A Spin
data.description:
The iShares Self-Driving EV and Tech ETF (IDRV), as the self-explanatory name indicates, is an index-benchmarked ETP from BlackRock Fund Advisors that gives investors exposure to "developed and emerging market companies that may benefit from growth and innovation in and around electric vehicles, battery technologies and autonomous driving technologies."

Introduced on April 16, 2019, IDRV tracks the NYSE FactSet Global Autonomous Driving and Electric Vehicle Index (NYFSAEV)(NYFSAEVT)(NYFSAEVN), so that's going to be our first port of call.

De-constructing the Underlying Index

By definition, the index is a "rules-based equity benchmark designed to track the performance of globally listed companies involved with Autonomous Driving and Electric Vehicles."

These rules are applied to six distinct categories of companies that cover a spectrum of EV and self-driving or autonomous vehicle technologies, from battery materials producers to car makers and self-driving software developers and hardware vendors.

These companies can be in any "one of 43 developed or emerging market countries", but in every single case, must derive more than half their revenues from goods and services related to EVs and autonomous vehicles. Their selection rests on two specific criteria - revenue exposure and their positioning and relationship to each other in the supply chain.

These filtered equities are then ordered by float-adjusting their market caps, and further rules are applied to this ordered list. No single entity can exceed a 4% weighting, and so as to avoid concentration in the technology side over the application side, the index also caps the aggregate weighting of these two (of six) categories at 25%. That leaves more room for upstream companies like Albemarle (ALB), a major lithium producer that would otherwise have no place in IDRV.

I'd describe IDRV as a fund that seeks to maximize exposure to these emergent technologies by identifying the ones most likely to benefit from market growth in the self-driving and EV segments.

IDRV and DRIV - Not Just Anagrams of Each Other!

One important aspect I'd like to introduce here is the surprisingly light overlap between IDRV and another, bigger fund called the Global X Autonomous & Electric Vehicles ETF (DRIV). You've probably come across it if you know IDRV, but these aren't redundant with each other, surprisingly enough.

What it implies is up to you to figure out, and I'd encourage you to do that because what looks like a pair of similar ETFs actually isn't.

When you see this...

...you'll understand what's causing this...

There's a much lower technology focus with IDRV, so you can't really call DRIV's run-up more strategic. Global X just chose to focus more on the consumer and enterprise end of these technologies rather than the support structure and supply chains underlying them that its iShares peer chose to focus on. This isn't chance. Each of these funds seeks to achieve a set of objectives that guides their price and total return trajectories.

Knowing what we know now, it's easier to separate the wheat from the chaff - the pros from the cons.

Pros of the iShares Self-Driving EV and Tech ETF

  • Exposure to technologies with long-term growth runways
  • Vertical exposure across two distinct supply chains - Electric and Autonomous Vehicles
  • Lower weighting for the more volatile technology sector
  • Better exposure to the Materials sector over DRIV's
  • Lower ER of 0.48% (DRIV - 0.68%)
  • 4Y average yield of 2.2% (DRIV - 1.39%)

Cons of IDRV

  • Operates in a small niche segment that has further subdivisions
  • Low AUM of under $140 million (DRIV - $320 million)
  • Weak performance relative to DRIV

I'll say it again. I don't hold it against IDRV for underperforming its peer. In fact, I don't see it as a peer at all. It's the same space, but we've just seen how different they are. It's also clear that a technology-heavy portfolio can go from outperformer to underperformer in a flash.

Who is IDRV Right For?

We've seen how this ETF is built, how it's performed in the past and how it's performing now, so what's left is to identify the type of investor this fund might appeal to the most.

  1. The emerging-technology investor (full-sized position or building up to it)
  2. The forward technologies speculator (smaller position)
  3. The dividend growth investor
  4. The global diversifier
  5. The lithium equity investor looking to vertically grow the portfolio

Now let's look at who this might not be the best option for:

  1. The income investor looking to maximize short-term and more frequent distributions
  2. The low volatility seeker - 27% annualized for both ETFs
  3. The investor looking to generate alpha over the broader cap-weighted equity pool

This is intended to be an educational primer on IDRV so I'm not making any recommendations, but I think it warrants a brief look at the macro headwinds and tailwinds for these industries.

The Bird's Eye View

In terms of high-level influencing factors, I'd say oil plays a very important role in the success of EVs.

In the U.S., the expiration of subsidies last year caused a significant dip in BEV sales. This could potentially have a long tail, and with oil and gas production being prioritized over non-conventional energy alternatives, the setup looks far from perfect.

Starkly in contrast, Europe is seeing strong growth in EVs, and December 2025 was a watershed month when BEV sales overtook gas-fueled car sales.

That's the EV side of it. On the autonomous driving technology side, we're still in the early days of this story, but Level 4 autonomy is quickly becoming a reality driven (literally) by the robotaxi industry. Several rollouts of L4 capabilities have been planned for later this year, so it could act as a multi-catalytic boost.

I don't mean to imply that EV technologies aren't seeing any movement. On the contrary, leaps in battery materials, power density, and other key developments continue to breach the surface into the public eye. The recent oil crisis could add even more momentum to these efforts, and these alternate avenues promise some really long growth runways.

I'd advise you to conduct an independent due diligence on IDRV if this looks interesting to you. There are tons of moving parts to an investment case for this niche ETF, so tread carefully and size your position in line with the risks involved.

This article answers these three questions about IDRV:

  • What type of investor is IDRV most suitable for?
  • What unique risks do IDRV investors face?
  • Does IDRV have any direct peers?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Micron: The Selloff Didn't Break The Setup
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Micron Technology (MU) is one of the companies that got me the setup that I like. It is hardly a month since I did an update on Micron last month. But I decided to do another update because two things have happened. First is that there is the ongoing Israel-US, Iran war that is still at its peak. And secondly, tech stocks seem to have been properly hammered as a result of this war. And Micron is at the forefront of that slip. But what is interesting is that the company is still printing record numbers. I mean the company released their 2Q 2026 Earnings in the middle of the war. And even after the realise, I note that the company is still guiding to even a higher guidance. I also note that the Growth grades and Valuation grades are improving. And as a result, I believe that the market is reacting to a mix of sell the news behaviour after massive run and an increasing capex. The sharp capex is screaming cycle risk to anyone who has lived through memory booms.

In my last piece, I wrote that MU is sitting in a pretty sweet spot. And that is still where my head is even if the market thinks otherwise. But here is the twist. The pullback actually improves the setup because a big chunk of the war is narrative driven. And then there is also the angle of position driven for investors that are profit taking. Meanwhile as I have mentioned, the factor grades show that the business is solid. And that is why I am comfortable reiterating a Strong Buy.

One of the reasons why I am reiterating a Strong Buy is that MU's earnings power is exploding faster than the market is willing to believe is sustainable. And yet the stocks is being priced like this is a one quarter miracle. Micron's revenue reached $23.86Bn in 2Q 2026 and also delivered record profitability. It is also important to mention that management guided that 3Q 2026 would hit $33.5Bn with gross margin around 81% and non-GAAP [EPS] at around $19.15. In my view, this is a complete new business for a capex intensive memory business.

Business Overview

Micron sits in a simple but powerful position in the AI stack. And this is because it sells memory and storage that end up everywhere. For instance, there is cloud servers, client devices, mobile, auto, etc. That kind of breadth matters because AI does not just sit in one place anymore. I say this because it's training in hyperscale data centers. It is also running inference everywhere. And memory content per system tends to rise when compute intensity increases.

What stood out in 2Q 2026 release is that every major segment of the business posted decent numbers. Cloud, core data center, mobile and client. Yet all these units posted higher than prior year. The bottomline is that MU is not waiting for AI. It is already being paid across its portfolio.

What has changed since Last Update

It is important to look at what changed since the last article I did on MU. So, two things changed.One is real and fundamental. And the other is real but the market is probably overstating it in the short term. The first change is that MU's capex story got bigger. Micron boosted its 2026 capital spending plan by $5Bn, bring its 2026 investment to over $25Bn. It also indicated spending rise again in 2027 with construction related costs expected to be more than $10Bn higher y/y. Honestly, that is not small. And this is where the market nervous about too much supply even if supply is not coming anytime soon. And I say this because memory has always moved in cycles. One can have a great quarter then somebody builds too much and then the party ends. So the capex headlines can definitely spark selling even when current fundamentals are absurdly strong.

The second change was a narrative shock. Google Research introduced TurbboQuant which says it can compress the key value cache memory used in LLM inference, cutting KV memory footprint by at least 6x while maintaining accuracy. Now, that announcement hit memory stocks like a brick. The WSJ described MU and other memory names selling off after TurboQuant with the fear of being " less memory needed" equals "less memory demand."

The question is whether there is anything special in all these narratives. And as for me, I do not see any. For sure, the earnings mattered to the stock slipping. But not because the quarter was weak. In fact the quarter was outrageous and the guidance was even louder. In my opinion, the stock slipped because the expectations were already extreme. And the earnings package brought forward the two things that make trades nervous. Capex and peak cycle talk. It is important to point out that according to MarchWatch, MU fell into the bear market territory after the sharp decline even as earnings estimates increase. And this makes Micron Technology the cheapest stock in the S&P500 on a FWD P/E basis at that moment.

Valuation

Even after the slip, the factor grades still look attractive. Growth is sitting at A+ and the valuation is sitting at B+. And this means that the fundamental machine is still running and the stock is now cheaper than it was when the market was euphoric. Let us start with the Growth grade. Well, I note that the revenue growth rate stands at 86% while the sector median sits at 10%. Additionally the FWD revenue growth rate is 87% while the sector median is expected at 10.7%. When I last analyzed Micron last month the Revenue growth rates were 45% [y/y] and 55% [FWD] respectively.

Now when it comes to valuation, MU looks relatively cheaper to the sector median. In fact the valuation grade has improved since I last looked at MU. FWD non-GAAP P/E stands at 5.57x while the sector median sits at 20.39x. This suggests a 72% discount to the sector median. On the other side, the FWD EV/EBITDA trades at 4.03x while the sector median trades at 12.33x. This suggests a 67% discount. And lastly, the FWD non-GAAP PEG of MU stands at 0.04x while the sector median stands at 0.82x. This of course suggests a 96% discount relative to the sector median. And this is what completely changes the game for Micron.

Compared to peers, Western Digital (WDC) trades at a FWD Non-GAAP P/E of 28.42x while its FWD Non-GAAP PEG 0.8x . On the other side, Seagate Technology (STX) trades at a FWD Non-GAAP P/E 27.8x of while its FWD Non-GAAP PEG 0.75x. And lastly, Sandisk Corp (SNDK) has its FWD non-GAAP P/E at 14.22x while its FWD non-GAAP PEG sits at 0.06x. At the end of the day, MU has the lowest of them all.

Micron's Key Risk

The biggest risk I see for Micron right now is the same old monster. That memory is cyclical. I mean we just say how things could turn quickly during the launch of TurboQuant. Of course capex step up is necessary for long term demand but it also feeds the fear that the industry will eventually overbuild and rest margins.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, the 2Q 2026 results were really good. I mean given they even came up with an increase in dividend which signals confidence. The stock slipping is explainable as I have demonstrated earlier. Capex fear, profit taking and software efficiency headline that hit the entire memory at once. The reason I am comfortable reiterating my Strong Buy rating is the disconnect. And that's because of the growth and valuation grades.

data.title: Bouncing Into April?
data.description:
Yesterday marked the best stock market bounce at the end of a quarter since September 2008. Far from an all-clear sign, bounces like that are most common during bear markets (shown below, courtesy of Bespoke).

Mean reversion has made some progress in the sell-off to date, but Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) is not parting with its cash pile just yet.

The pile grew from ~$128B at the end of 2019 to a peak of $381.7B in Q3 2025 (dark blue above) - a near sevenfold increase - as Buffett sold stakes in Apple (AAPL) and Bank of America (BAC) while finding few acquisitions worth current pricing. The Q4 2025 figure dipped slightly to $373.3B, and the most recent filing (Q1 2025 in the 10-Q) shows cash at $343B.

The Middle East war is making matters worse, and we hope for a quick resolution. But when it comes to markets and liquidity strains, problems go far deeper and will not be solved by an end to oil constraints.

Market bottoms don’t come while the masses are still feeling optimistic enough to buy every dip. As retired market analyst Wally Deemer wisely quipped: “When it’s time to buy, you won’t want to.” That is when cash will be king, once more.

Disclosure: No positions

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Dave & Buster's Entertainment: Why I'm Waiting And Downgrading My Price Target
data.description:
I've been covering Dave & Buster's (PLAY) for a while - trying to essentially determine when is a good time to enter this entertainment-related investment. It's clear to me that despite everything and all of the negatives which are certainly here, alongside risks, which are also certainly present, the company can be quite profitable. Not lately - the company's FCF in fact did go negative for FY 2025, and has been negative before. But what I am saying is that, barring something like the COVID-19 pandemic, this company is one that generates a fair bit of revenue. And when you generate growing revenue, which this company does, what you're looking at is finding ways to turn that into good profit. And honestly, the company is doing both of those things.

It's quite obvious even to a layman that any company that struggles to eke out an operating income that's significantly higher than 2020, when the 2025 revenue is almost 50% higher, is going through a bit of margin and profitability issues. That's fine - we can account and discount for that. The problem comes when investors do not discount for this pressure, which is what I believe investors have not been doing as of late. It's why I was relatively bearish on the company's share price, and gave it a "HOLD" in my last article, found here. It's also why I was not surprised at the underperformance, even though the degree of underperformance was something that exceeded my overall expectations.

So in this article, I will be addressing the company's recent results, which are causing a bit of a surge in the stock price following the low levels. Both SA and Wall Street have been positive on this company for a while - I meanwhile believe that Quant has had the right way of it for some time - rating the company anaemic or negative in growth, profit, and momentum, resulting in a negative rating or at the very least a neutral one of "wait and see".

Let's see what the results can do to convince us that things are about to turn around.

Dave & Buster's - The risk materialized much as I expected

In my latest article I gave clear guidance that the primary risk to the company is underperformance relative to targets and goals, and the impact this would have on valuation. I believe this is what happened up until now. Even if the company surged yesterday, which it did, and it surges again today as a result of macro and other factors, it likely won't recapture even close to the ground that it needs to really "get" where some investors and analyst have their PT's. I say this because the high-end PT for the company comes to $46, and while I do not doubt that in the right circumstances the company can achieve this, those circumstances seem very far from what we have today as of the year-end of 2025 and early 2026.

Basically, the market is "waiting for a turnaround" here. I argue in part that this expectation may be based on the somewhat erroneous assumption that PLAY could recover to previous levels of profitability. I view this as being extremely unlikely for all of the reasons mentioned in my previous article. Costs, margins and the way people attend these sorts of entertainment venues seem to be changing.

Consumers are also becoming increasingly price-sensitive as the general state of the economy, their incomes, and inflation worsen. All of these factors need to be considered when you're looking at a company that asks people for discretionary, rather than staple spending.

The initial state of the results for 4Q25 seems like a negative one, and doesn't really make much sense. The company missed both on EPS, and missed on revenues - so why, in pre-trade and yesterday, was the stock and is the stock surging?

A revenue decrease is not a good sign - neither is a store sales decrease, and a net loss per share is the very definition of "not good" for a stock company. Even the adjusted EBITDA was down, and the company's annual results largely mirror its 4Q25 results - it was not great.

It's clear that we need to look beyond these numbers to find why the stock is up. On the operational-strategic side, the company opened new stores and remodeled over 15 stores in 2025. Since the 2023 remodeling plan, over 50 D&B locations have therefore been remodeled. Further, the company also expanded its international locations through its franchise model. It's still only very low - four in total - but its a star.

The company also isn't in dire straits financially, with liquidity and revolver available at over $480M. However, it would be completely wrong to say that the fundamental situation is not worrying. In less than 10 years, the company has moved from less than $400M in net debt to more than $3.5B in net debt.

Much of this increase is related to the Main event acquisition in 2022, but the COVID-19 pandemic also necessitated massive draws of the revolvers. Obviously, the recent surge of remodelling is being funded mostly by debt as well, which leads to concern about how exactly the company is going to eventually pay down this veritable mountain of debt - relative to a 2025A negative FCF, and a typical FCF of about $200M per year.

From what I can take away from management's commentary and earnings, the positives are limited to improvements in marketing and promotions, sharpening lineups, and implementing new games, while also improving management. Growing same-store sales alongside FCF is the priority - but to me, these go without saying. The management forecast of $100M In FCF is to me, neither impressive nor a justification for the recent surge in earnings. The reason is that as interest expense has developed, and has been filed in 2025, that wouldn't even be enough to cover the company's annual interest expense.

It's my view that most of the positives reported or expected are part of the everyday operations of a successful company. They in no way offer justification for a reversal here. Even at the estimated recovery of EPS in 2026, the management guidance provides at most $0.3/share in AEPS. That would, if we look at today's share price, represent a P/E multiple of 36x. I do not need to tell you that this is not attractive to me, given the company's leverage.

Even the most positive operating income and AEPS expectations, which by the way would require the company to double/triple its current estimate, comes to about $0.7/share. Even if you bought the company below $11/share, this would represent buying the company at close to 16x P/E, which is in line with its 5-year P/E average. And the company has not even proved as of yet that it can actually do this.

You may read between the lines (or indeed, on the lines) that my view of the company here is negative. I believe the past 8-9 years of debt-fuelled expansion and remodelling have left the company in a "debt hole" that it will, at the very least, struggle to get out of, even at the best assumptions.

Furthermore, if you look at the company's average interest rate on debt, it's at 8.5%. This suggests that the market is, rightly, viewing the company as somewhat problematic.

Further, there is absolutely no sign of this debt burden going down. I had hoped for some fundamental changes in 2026, some forecast of massive improvement, but it goes to show that I can be over-optimistic as well. It's clear to me that no such massive improvement relative to normalized earnings is forthcoming. In fact, increasing CapEx and increasing interest burdens necessitate the company to draw further debt. What I consider to be even worse (in terms of capital allocation decisions), is to use at least part of this debt to buy back shares.

While I normally don't object to companies buying back shares at low valuations, my argument is that this isn't a low valuation, because the company very much consists of debt at this time. Leveraged share buybacks are, in my view, one of the worst decisions the company can make here, further increasing leverage and weakening the balance sheet.

Unless the company has some "master plan" in mind for turning this around, I see a very high likelihood of the company being at best compressed in terms of OI and earnings, where a $0.2-$0.3 is the best the company can hope to achieve.

I therefore start 2026E with a negative outlook on D&B/PLAY as a business.

Dave & Buster's - Valuation shows it's still being traded too highly

The company surged due to what I believe to be positive EBITDA/FCF guidance. The problem that I see is that even in the scenario where this is achieved or done, the company is today in a position to be considered overvalued. While the company did beat some 4Q25 estimates, I believe the market is overreacting to what amounts to little more than marketing promises in terms of menu enhancements and ongoing, already-present turnaround and renovation efforts. There is some value to the new 60-day unlimited play pass, but it's too early to say what impact this would have on the actual value of the company.

In my last article, I gave the company a PT of $11/share. I now say that this was too high. While the company is incredibly likely to surge past $11/share today, I say that the company needs to be rebased for an even higher debt load than I expected. Even in the event of a recovery of the estimated EBITDA, a $500M EBITDA would be no higher than what was achieved in 2024 - but at a debt load that is materially higher than during that year. I also estimate the company's interest expenses to rise.

I'm cutting my target to PT of $8.8/share, which comes to a 15x P/E to a discounted 2027-2028E AEPS of about $0.6/share. I believe I'm still allowing myself to see value here, and be somewhat positive.

But I want management to show me some of the results of the things they are promising first - and as things stand, this is not what we have here. The company's current net debt of about 4x to EBITDA can be further qualified by a declining EBIT. Even in the forecast, the company struggles to generate FCF to handle this. Nothing in the same store trends, declining in 4Q, suggests that this is likely to turn around. S&P itself has noted that the interest coverage is now less than 1.7x.

The downgrading is indicative of significant risk. PLAY is now at B-. I believe Wall Street estimates and ratings imply a willingness to "play with PLAY" - I am not willing to take that risk.

My rating remains hold, and I thusly cut my PT to $8.7/share. This makes me, to date and compared to other S&P Global analysts, the most negative one out there, with the lowest being at $13/share. Note, however, that even with that, 6 analysts out of 8 are at "HOLD", not at "BUY".

Risks & Considerations

The primary risk to Dave & Buster's here continues to be underperformance relative to its targets and goals. The question is whether the company has "gone too far" and whether a turnaround simply takes too long or is too unlikely. That is the primary risk - not a bankruptcy risk, but a risk that would end up with common shareholders either wiped out in some way, or with shares being made worth so little that it amounts to something similar.

I maintain that this risk at this time is very much there, and that the valuation is not taking this into account. I therefore downgrade my PT.

Thesis

Dave & Buster's is not an uninteresting company. I believe that at the right valuation, and with the right indicators of a turnaround, this company could be a compelling investment. Without a doubt, the company is being underestimated in terms of how a positive return to profit could impact valuation - but the company needs to bring proof of that before I'd be even slightly willing to take that risk.

But I'd want that return to profit to be made clear before I'd invest here. Based on this, I give the company a PT of $8.7/share, making a "HOLD" rating the relevant one here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 2 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 2/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $8.7/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026.

data.title: Micron: Great Businesses Can Be Bad Trades (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Past coverage and price movement

Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) is back in the headlines, and it’s not so positive this time around. Since Google’s announcement of TurboQuant in a blog post in late March, Micron is down around 20%, according to Seeking Alpha, along with peers like Western Digital (WDC) and Sandisk (SNDK). I discuss the announcement in more detail in my last coverage on Sandisk, so I won’t go into details now.

Citi also reduced its price target ("PT") on the stock from $510 to $425, and said in an investor note on Tuesday that the announcement has led to lower DRAM prices, which have declined 6% since Micron’s earnings report earlier in March.

“At face value, a new efficiency technique such as TurboQuant reduces AI compute and memory cost per inquiry, but it also further unlocks usage, which ultimately increases compute/memory demand. We see the computing demand as a function of (1) individual computing needs and (2) usage. Demand for compute/memory shrinking will depend on the differential between how much (1) is coming down via innovations and (2) is going up as a factor of lower computing cost. Historically, we think cheaper technology has mostly increased the demand for more technology. We see AI as no different."

But even prior to Google’s announcement, I’ve grown increasingly skeptical of Micron’s run over the past two months, which prompted me to downgrade the stock to a hold in early February and again in mid March. The stock is down over 18% since my February call, and down another 23.5% since my March call.

Back in February, I saw that the risk-reward turned less attractive with the stock struggling to break the resistance around $455. While the AI-driven memory story remains intact, I saw a period of consolidation coming as memory pricing risks normalizing after a sharp upswing. Nothing justified chasing the stock at that level, and the only reason I didn’t downgrade Micron to a Sell, at the time, was thinking it could still have some fuel in the tank following the presentation at the Wolfe Research Auto, Auto Tech, and Semiconductor 2026 Conference. Sure enough, the stock was up briefly following the presentation, but has come down significantly since. At the risk of repeating myself, I said the following.

Fast forward to my mid-March call, right before earnings, and the situation didn’t look any brighter for Micron. Sure, on paper, the market should’ve reacted positively after the company’s beat across the board, but that didn’t happen. Instead, Micron crashed and has continued to do so since then, down another 10% on Monday alone. While the stock was up around 5% on Tuesday as part of the broader market rebound, and up another 5.3% on Wednesday, I don’t think momentum is fully back into the stock, and think the stock is surging strictly in sympathy with the overall market.

Micron remains under its EMA50 and EMA21, which are both acting as resistance at current levels.

Earnings review

EPS came in at $12.20, significantly above consensus at $9.31 and revenue at $23.86 billion, against above consensus at $20.07, up from $8.05 billion in a year ago quarter. The company expects 3Q revenue at $33.5 billion, implying a 200% year over year growth, and above the $24.3 billion consensus, and EPS at $19.15, again above consensus at $12.05. GAAP gross margin more than doubled year-over-year to 74.4%, up from 36.8% and significantly higher than 56% in the prior quarter. Net income surged to $13.8 billion ($12.07 per share), compared to $1.58 billion ($1.41 per share) a year ago. Growth was driven by strong demand across segments, with cloud memory revenue up over 160% to $7.75 billion, while the mobile and client business more than tripled to $7.71 billion. The selloff that followed was a classic case of selling the news, and I wasn’t surprised.

The most important takeaway for me is the capex raise. Micron revised FY26 capex up to above $25 billion, and then guided FY27 construction spend up over $10 billion year-over-year on top of higher equipment spend, meaning free cash flow will compress even as earnings soar, and the market tends to punish that dynamic once it becomes visible.

My margin ceiling argument also holds up. The company’s CFO Mark Murphy essentially confirmed what I flagged in my pre-earnings coverage. At 81% gross margin, the incremental gain from any further price increase is mathematically shrinking, and easy expansion is behind Micron.

The next leg requires HBM4 mix, sustained ASP strength, and cost execution all firing simultaneously. Another takeaway is non-HBM margins being actually higher than high bandwidth memory, or HBM, which the company’s CEO, Sanjay Mehrotra, confirmed on the earnings call.

“it is correct that the margins for non-HBM today are higher than HBM margins. Demand for HBM of course continues to be strong. We have not updated the numbers that we had provided last in terms of the outlook for the HBM TAM. Of course, the demand for DDR5, LP, and HBM, all of them continue to be strong in the data centers. We, of course, continue to manage the mix of the business as the data center AI demand continues to grow. And as I said earlier, we, of course, outside of data center, we are very much focused on making sure that we are maintaining relevant share in our key other market segments as well.”

Micron is being rewarded more DRAM than on its flagship AI product, which cuts against the narrative that HBM mix shift is a pure margin tailwind. Still, I acknowledge that the business itself is executing at a level that's hard to argue with, and HBM4 supply sold out through 2026 under long-term agreements is real demand visibility, especially since DRAM and NAND remain supply-constrained beyond 2026.

Why I’m downgrading

The Micron business is exceptional, but the stock already paid for exceptional and is now being asked to pay for sustained exceptional. I think easy money in Micron’s upside has been made, and I’m downgrading the stock to a sell at current levels. My sell isn’t against Micron, but against the price.

Micron’s price action over the past few weeks tells the story better than anything else: this is a stock that already priced in a near-perfect memory upcycle, and is now starting to unwind as expectations reset. The stock is now down over 17% over the past month, and another 8% over the past five days. Still, the stock remains in the green year to date, up over 18%, outperforming the market, with the S&P 500 down around 4.6% during the same period.

While the long-term thesis remains intact, what I think has changed now is the timing and expectations embedded in the stock. The core issue here is that Micron has transitioned from being an under-owned AI beneficiary to a crowded trade. The narrative around HBM, AI-driven DRAM demand, and tight supply conditions pushed the stock aggressively higher, but the market is now starting to question how much of that upside is already reflected in the stock. We’re also seeing early signs of what typically happens at this stage of the memory cycle, which typically start with 1. expectations peaking before fundamentals fully catch up, 2. marginal upside becoming harder to justify, and 3. any softness, whether in pricing, guidance, or geopolitical uncertainty, gets punished.

While we’re on the latter, rising geopolitical tensions, volatility in commodities, and uncertainty around rates are pushing the market into a more risk-off posture, and cyclicals like memory tend to get hit first in that environment.

While my long-term bullish thesis remains, I think the risk-reward at current levels is unfavorable. HBM demand remains strong, and AI-driven memory content is increasing, but none of that is new information. The market knows this, and more importantly, it already paid for it.

Valuation and what’s next:

On the surface, Micron’s multiples look attractive, with forward P/E of 5.85x against a sector median of 21.28x, a forward EV/EBITDA of 4.23x against 12.71x, and a PEG ratio of 0.04x that is essentially screaming value. But deeply discounted forward multiples on a memory name are not a gift, and the market knows that. I think this is the market's way of saying it doesn't fully trust that those forward earnings will materialize or be sustained.

EV/Sales TTM comes in at 6.46x against a sector median of 3.13x, a 106% premium. Price/Sales TTM is similarly elevated at 6.52x versus 3.11x, and Price/Book TTM is 5.26x, 63% above the sector median. These confirm what the price action has been telling us: Micron got ahead of itself. I'm not recommending investors sell at a loss, but I am saying that buying at current levels is a risk I wouldn't take.

Micron is trading at $353.59, well below both the EMA21 at $386.97 and the EMA50 at $381.73, both of which are now sloping downward and acting as resistance rather than support, as I previously mentioned, with RSI at 41.10 not yet in oversold territory, but not bullish either. The stock peaked at $471 in early March and has been in a consistent pattern of lower highs and lower lows since.

The EMA200 at $273.18 is the next meaningful support level if the EMAs above fail to hold, and that's a scenario I wouldn't rule out if the broader market stays risk-off. What I want to see before reconsidering my sell is a reclaim of the EMA50 on meaningful volume, followed by a successful retest of that level as support. Until that happens, any bounce, including today's 5 move, looks more like a relief rally in sympathy with the broader market than a genuine reversal in trend.

The fundamentals are strong, and the cycle is real, but great businesses can still be bad trades.

data.title: Costamare: Protected In The Near Term, Pressured In The Medium Term
data.description:
Introduction

Costamare (CMRE) looks like a stable, contract backed business on the surface, but if we look deeper, we will find that the revenue is tied to the cyclicality of global trade. Currently, the company has a revenue backlog of about $3.4 billion with a 4.5-year duration. This backlog provides stability in the near term, but in the medium term, the company will still face the renegotiation risk from its customers. The revenue growth supercycle is already behind us. Due to a weak global trade outlook, geopolitical tension and rising supply of the fleets, the company can face renegotiation risk in the medium term. But the question is what expectations are factored into the stock price of the company.

What is Costamare?

Costamare is a maritime asset owner. Its core business is owning containerships and chartering them with crew and operational support. Generally, the company charters its vessels under long term contracts in which daily rates are predetermined. These multiyear contracts have created a sizeable revenue backlog. It provides near term visibility. I am specifically mentioning near term visibility because, on paper, this backlog looks attractive and can create a narrative of stable income, but that is not the complete picture.

Costamare’s business is linked to global trade and vessel supply and demand dynamics, which makes the overall business cyclical. So, even though customers have signed a long term contract, in a weak market, they might come to renegotiate the daily rates, or in the worst case, they might default.

Currently, the total revenue backlog is about $3.4 billion, and its TEU-weighted duration is 4.5 years. The TEU weighted average duration is calculated by giving more importance to the size of the fleet because the company's bigger ships generate more revenue. But as I mentioned before, we can’t certainly say what will happen in 4.5 years after considering the current geopolitical situation.

Revenue drivers of the core business are charter rates, fleet size, contract duration, and utilisation.

Leasing containerships is not the only business of Costamare. In 2023, it became the controlling shareholder of Neptune Maritime Leasing. In this business, Costamare buys the ships and gives them to the customer without any crew or operational responsibility in exchange for a fixed payment. This type of contract is a bareboat charter.

The nature of both businesses is capital-intensive, which is funded through debt most of the time. Also, almost 75% revenue generation of Costamare is concentrated in the top five customers.

Currently, the company owns 79 containerships, and the idle fleet rate is at 0.5%. The idle fleet rate is near its 10-year low. The time charter rate for the last two years has been resilient.

Overall, the company’s business appears stable because of long term contracts, but they are completely tied to the unpredictable cycles of global trade and the financial strength of its concentrated customer base.

Financials

Costamare’s revenue growth cycle is closely tied to the charter rates. The same trend is visible in the company’s last five years of revenue growth. It was majorly influenced by the sharp supercycle in charter rates and fleet expansion.

Once the charter rates started normalizing, the same change can be observed in the company’s revenue growth. So, the recent performance shows that the company is transitioning financially toward slow contract-backed revenue generation after sharp growth and normalization of charter rates.

We can observe the same story in the number of companies. The company’s 5-year CAGR is 14%, while due to the normalizing charter rates, the 3-year CAGR is -8%.

Even the company’s profitability followed a similar pattern. The gross profit and EBITDA benefited from strong operating leverage during the upcycle and remained resilient compared to pre-cycle levels with the help of cost discipline. So, overall operating profitability growth and margins both remained stable. But on the other hand, because of asset sales and financing effects such as rising interest expenses, the net profit margin and growth were both volatile.

In the last 2 years, Costamare has started debt repayment, which has reduced total debt by almost 43% compared to FY2023. So, the company’s balance sheet has strengthened meaningfully, which can be seen in the leverage ratio. Reducing debt also reduces the interest expense. Even after that, the net profit margin still remained depressed because of the impact of the discontinued operations.

At the same time, cashflow conversion remains solid but reflects the cyclical and capital-intensive nature of the business, with variability due to investment activity and market conditions.

Overall, I think the financial growth from a sharp rate-driven cycle is behind us, and now we are looking at the normalised growth of the company.

If we look at the financial scorecard as a traditional company, it shows that the financials are above average, but once we adjust it for the cyclicality, it shows that the financials are in the average category. Cyclicality adjustment is important because the overall business model is cyclical.

What will happen next?

As I have mentioned multiple times in this article, the growth of Costamare is closely linked to the global trades and containership market. So, understanding the outlook of both is essential.

The global trade market is expected to grow at a slower pace compared to the post-pandemic demand surge. In 2025, the global trade market had grown by 4.1%. But the growth is expected to decline 2.6% YoY in 2026 due to demand normalization and structural pressure due to geopolitical issues.

At the same time, container demand is expected to grow at 1.7%-3% while fleet supply is expected to increase at 3%-3.6% in 2026. This imbalance can create a structural pressure on the charter rates in the medium term. Because generally the charter contracts are long term, and even if the supply will increase in 2026, its effect on charter will be visible at the end of 2027 or the start of 2028.

However, due to geopolitical problems in the Middle East and the Red Sea crisis, the vessels are forced to take a longer path, which has increased the voyage time and reduced the fleet's availability. As a result, in the near term, we can see a surge in charter rates.

Overall, even though there could be a surge in charter rates, in the medium term, we can expect structural pressure on charter rates.

In the case of Costamare, I believe that for the next two years, assuming that the current charter rates can stay in the same range is reasonable because fleet employment for FY2026 and FY2027 is 96% and 92%, respectively. So, almost all fleets will be booked under pre-decided charter rates, and even if the rate starts declining in FY2027, it won’t impact the company’s financials immediately.

To calculate the estimated revenue of Costamare, we need to integrate contracted backlog, incremental contracts, incremental revenue from new fleet additions, and cycle normalization.

Let’s start with the company’s contracted revenue backlog because it will give us the base revenue from the existing operation. Currently, Costamare’s contracted revenue backlog is $3.5 billion, which has a remaining duration of 4.5 years.

As I have mentioned earlier, even though it is labelled as contracted revenue, it is not certain in the long term because any sharp changes in market conditions can lead to renegotiations about all contract conditions. But we can certainly integrate it into the next one or two years' revenue estimate because the changes in market conditions won’t have an immediate impact on the contract conditions. So, the current revenue backlog can give us the annual base revenue of about $756 million for the next two years.

Recently, Costamare locked in fresh contracted revenue of about $940 million. Under this contract, it has fixed 12 vessels, and the TEU-weighted average duration of these is six years. In short, this fresh contracted revenue can give incremental revenue of about $155 million. This is already included in the base revenue of $756 million.

The company is adding six newbuild vessels. It is fully backed by long term contracts. But it will start contributing to the revenue in FY2027. The size of each ship is approximately 3,100 TEU. As per 2024 data, the average per-day rate of a 3,000 TEU vessel is approximately $25,000. According to the investor presentation, charter rates have been almost the same since 2024, and in the near term, the price changes seem unlikely because the rates are already locked in long term contracts for these vessels. So, if we assume 350 working days, all six ships together can add almost $52 million to total revenue.

Some of these ships are expected to be delivered in FY2028. But we don’t know exact numbers, that is why I am assuming that 50% will be delivered in FY2027 and the remaining in FY2028. So, I am assuming 50% of the total potential of these 6 ships, that is $26 million, can be realized in FY2027 and the complete revenue potential of $52 million can contribute every year, FY2028 onwards. However, the revenue contribution of FY2028 depends on the charter rate, as I believe the charter rate might experience structural pressure in the medium term.

Another growth factor for Costamare is Neptune Maritime Leasing. Total deployed capital to this business has increased from $123 million in FY2024 to $180 million in FY2025. It gives an average capital deployed of $150 million, and the business has earned $31.2 million in revenue from that capital. So, the total yield on the capital is 20% at the current scale.

The company mentioned in the latest conference call that total investment in this platform in future will be more than $665 million. With $180 million already deployed, the remaining capital base is $485 million. We do not know the time period of the deployment, but if we consider the historical annual deployment, which is on average $60 million per year, the remaining capital base will be deployed over the next 8 years. So, in FY2026 and FY2027, with the 20% yield, we can expect the total revenue of about $48 million and $60 million, respectively, from this business.

To estimate EBITDA margin, rather than taking a simple average of EBITDA margin of the last 10 years, I am only taking the average of margins of normalized years and excluding supercycle years because we are assuming that, according to revenue estimates and global trade outlook, the next two years are going to be normalized years.

The average of the normalised years’ EBITDA margin is 66%. So I am assuming EBITDA margin could be 66% in FY2026 and FY2027.

Valuation

As I have mentioned, FY2026 and FY2027 could be the normalized years, same as FY2025, because even if the outlook of global trades is not positive and the world is facing a lot of geopolitical issues, the changes in charter rates will not impact Costamare’s contracts immediately. So, if the next two years are assumed normalized years as FY2025, we can assume that the EV/EBITDA multiples could be the same as FY2025.

With EVEBITDA multiple of 5.06x, the company’s fair value would be about $1.87 billion for FY2027. It shows 5% price decline from the current market capitalization of $1.96 billion. If we adjust the returns with the company’s dividend yield, the total returns of the investor would be about -2%. All these calculations are as per the current market capitalization.

Risks

At first glance, Costamare’s huge backlog and long term contracts create a picture of long term, predictable, stable revenue. However, the business model tells us financials could be volatile. As I have mentioned earlier, if there is a sharp correction in the charter rates or the global trade conditions, customers of the company can renegotiate conditions again.

Looking at the current outlook of the global trades, fleet supply and geopolitical conditions, the charter rates could decline in the medium term. Due to the long term contracts, I think the company is protected from the changes in charter rate in the near term. But if there is a share correction in charter rates in the near term, the customers can renegotiate the conditions of the contract in the medium term, which can result in decreased revenue.

The company’s 75% of revenue is generated from the top five customers. So, even if the geopolitical conditions start affecting the financials of any one customer, it could have a substantial impact on the financials of Costamare. And because of concentrated revenue, the company have low power in renegotiations.

Conclusion

Overall, Costamare looks stable at first glance, and honestly, for the next 1–2 years it might actually behave like that because of long term contracts and high fleet utilization. But if we look a little deeper, the business is still cyclical in nature and depends on what happens with charter rates and global trade. The supercycle is already behind us and now we are entering a more normalized phase where growth and returns are not going to be that exciting.

Because of this, I think Hold rating makes more sense. There is no major downside visible right now due to backlog support and dividends, but at the same time, upside is also limited. Even after including dividends, the total return comes around -2%, which is not very attractive.

So if you already hold the stock, you can continue holding it. But taking a fresh position right now doesn’t make much sense, there are better opportunities available where risk-reward looks more favourable.

data.title: RWL: Revenue-Weighting Is Likely To Outperform
data.description:
The Invesco S&P 500 Revenue ETF (RWL), launched on 02/19/2008 and managed by Invesco Capital Management LLC, offers differentiated exposure to large-cap stocks based on a revenue weighting approach. It has ~$8 billion in AUM and charges an expense ratio of 0.39%.

The ETF's revenue-based approach reduces concentration in mega-cap stocks and interest-rate-sensitive sectors. That has been useful in the past and it's likely to keep proving useful in the near term. Shifts in inflation and interest rate expectations are likely to hurt market-cap-weighted funds more and, therefore, cause RWL to outperform.

Methodology

RWL tracks the S&P 500 Revenue-Weighted Index, which starts with the S&P 500 as its available universe to select stocks based on a revenue bias. More specifically, it filters by only picking the positive-revenue stocks and then weights them by revenue size. The larger a company's share of the index's total trailing four-quarter revenue, the higher its weight.

Each stock has a 5% cap and rebalancing is done on a quarterly basis, effective after the close on the third Friday of March, June, September, and December.

This approach is significantly different than the usual market-cap-weighted exposure you get from the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY). This can result in a different risk profile and portfolio.

Portfolio

The top 10 names account for 23.4% of the portfolio, which is meaningfully lower than SPY's 36.3% allocation. The top stock only has 4.1%, which indicates low idiosyncratic risk.

More importantly, the sector composition reflects a meaningful defensive exposure to the market. We got Health Care at almost 20%, Consumer Defensive at 11.6%, and Utilities at 2.7% (~35% total). When growth cools, which underpins the current macro narrative, these sectors usually hold up better than the rest. SPY allocates around 17.5% to those sectors. The exposure to interest-rate sensitive equities is also lower here, around 18% (Technology and Communication); SPY has a very strong bias towards those stocks right now (43% of its portfolio).

Performance

Over the long run, RWL has underperformed market-cap weighting as represented by SPY, but outperformed the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP). This makes it appear as the sweet spot between the two more common weighting methodologies. It's worth noting that RWL has tracked SPY more closely than RSP, highlighting the strong correlation between the revenue and market cap factors.

That being said, RWL's methodology is anything but redundant for long-term investing. Performance on a risk-adjusted basis may have also been similar, but RWL's worst year was represented by the smallest loss, with its best year being almost the same as those of the rest. With the total return differential relative to SPY not being significantly wide and historically inconsistent, the opportunity cost for the benefit of lower single-name and sector concentration is not that meaningful.

In the last six months, RWL has illustrated how that lower concentration risk can be useful when macro risks are heightened and market sentiment weakens. It outperformed both SPY and RSP on a total return basis.

The more aggressive level of exposure to growth sectors and holdings explains SPY's underperformance. Mega-cap exposure has been particularly a drag as the market seemed to have reassessed the prospects of the AI infrastructure buildout. RSP has done meaningfully better than SPY but still underperformed RWL. Although RSP represents even lower single-name concentration risk than RWL due to its equal-weighting approach, it has the drawback of not having as strong a bias towards defensive sectors like Healthcare, Consumer Discretionary, and Utilities (~25%).

Outlook

The oil supply shock that followed the war in Iran and the subsequent closing of the Straits of Hormuz has rekindled inflation fears. In the last FOMC meeting, the Fed maintained the policy rate, but highlighted the elevated inflation and inflation expectations. Powell also tied the shift in these expectations for the near-term outlook to the supply disruption in the Middle East.

The market has quickly repriced the interest rate path since the war started. Just one month ago, the 30-Day Fed Funds futures prices implied a 23.3% probability for a cut in the Federal Funds rate. As of March 31, the probability has dropped to 0%.

Aside from the fact that RWL can be an attractive way to remain exposed to the market for the long run, as the evidence would suggest, it's also very relevant right now. A shift in interest rate expectations can punish sectors such as Information Technology and Communication more than the others due to the higher equity price sensitivity to rates. As I already said, SPY is significantly more exposed to those sectors than RWL. At the same time, RWL's higher concentration in Healthcare and Consumer Defensive makes it more insulated from the current market sentiment.

There is another parallel narrative to consider as well. Mega-cap stocks, especially those tied to the AI infrastructure buildout, are suffering from decreased market enthusiasm. YTD, the Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF (MAGS) has fallen by 16% on a total return basis. About one-third of SPY's portfolio is exposed to those stocks and that implies a meaningful drag right now. Even if the Iran war wasn't in the picture, the ever-increasing CapEx by hyperscalers has made investors skeptical of the potential payoff's magnitude and timing.

Risks

Investors should be mindful of the potential for an opportunity cost here, though. The situation in the Middle East is very volatile, and it's unclear how much further it can escalate or when it will start de-escalating. Although it's unlikely to be easy to resolve, the oil supply disruption has already been priced in, judging from the consolidation phase the ProShares K-1 Free Crude Oil ETF (OILK) entered two weeks ago.

Any strong hint that efforts to improve the supply are bearing fruit and could meaningfully bring oil prices down could make RWL underperform SPY; its exposure to the Energy sector is higher at 7.61%.

Conclusion

Overall, RWL is an attractive way to be exposed to equities for the long run, but also provides a less risky way for such exposure in the current regime. While I cannot ignore that SPY and market-cap-weighted ETFs have their use during a healthy bullish market sentiment, they have been underperforming for a while and I expect that to continue.

Do you agree with my thesis? Let me know below. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Visa: A Rare Discount On A World Class Compounder
data.description:
Visa (V) is one of my favourite holdings, a company I’ve been long since 2016. While I’m a buy and hold kind of investor, I can say that V would be one of the very last holdings I would liquidate if I had to raise cash, it’s a forever company in my book and one I plan to add to in broader market drawbacks or whenever the opportunity presents itself as the market misprices the stock. I’m saying this as I have a strong belief this company is riding the wave of a massive growth opportunity related to the global payment ecosystem transitioning from primarily cash to digital.

Visa is one of the most lucrative network and toll booth systems on the planet, and it’s a company that has continued delivering strong growth since becoming a publicly traded company almost twenty years ago.

Investment Thesis

My investment thesis is essentially captured in the illustration below. Rarely do we as investors get the opportunity to buy Visa at a 20% off the most recent high. That is because it’s a stable business that keeps churning out forecasted growth, while often exceeding consensus estimates. It’s a business with a very high ROIC riding the global trend of transitioning from cash to cashless, there is a lot to like about this business.

When the company is trading well below it’s most recent high, it also means it’s the first time I’ve covered the company where I can void the premium valuation.

Visa’s valuation comes in as follows:

  • Price / Earning forward of 23.3
  • Price / Earning forward 1Y of 20.6
  • Price / Earning 5Y average of 34.2
  • Price / Earning 5Y mean of 34.9

The numbers above are fairly telling, at least when measured on a P/E multiple, Visa appears quite interesting.

  • Price / Sales forward of 12.9
  • Price / Sales forward 1Y of 11.6
  • Price / Sales 5Y average of 17.4

This is a 78% gross margin, 67% operating margin and 33.7% ROIC company. These are juicy numbers, and I’m thrilled to see the company once again trade at a discount to its long-term average.

Visa is forecasted to grow at just above 12-13% clip for the coming 3 years on EPS level and 10-11.5% on topline. It really is hard not to pinch my own arm considering how Visa can grow into its valuation prompting the stock price to rise.

Visa is faced with regulatory risk, as has been the case for 10+ years due to its duopoly/oligopoly status, and there probably comes a day where that reality will catch up to the company in some form or another, but this is a company that grew FCF 270% over the past decade, while growing net income at 201% at the same time frame.

It’s evident I’m a Visa bull, and you should therefore take my bias into account when reading this, but in my book, this is a very strong buying opportunity for the long-term compounder.

Past Coverage

As mentioned earlier, I’ve been long Visa since 2016, but only covered the company twice here on Seeking Alpha. Both my prior pieces were bullish, same as this, but the company hasn’t faired well as per my two prior articles compared to the S&P500, not least due to the recent pullback while the S&P500 haven’t suffered to the same extent.

If we zoom out and disregard my poor timing, then Visa has outperformed the tech and top heavy S&P500 index on a 10Y horizon. Zooming in on a 3-5Y horizon and the index starts outperforming V, and on a 1Y horizon the difference is substantial with the S&P500 index up 13.6% and V down 12.6%, but this is what creates a buying opportunity.

The cornerstone of my thesis in prior articles concern themselves around the following, and those convictions remain live and well today, as they have since 2016.

  • The global society at large transitioning to cashless transactions
  • A consistently solid management team
  • A continued upwards trend in company financials, growing cash flow, revenue and operating margin.

Recent Financial & Business Development

Visa is one of those companies where predictability in terms of financial performance is quite high, which is unusual for companies, especially ones where growth is consistently double digits.

Visa reported its Q1 results on 29th Jan 2026, here are some of the highlights

  • Revenue of $10.9 billion, 15% YoY growth (beat of $211m vs consensus estimates)
  • Non-GAAP EPS of $3.17, 15% YoY growth (beat of $0.03 vs consensus estimates)
  • Free cash flow of $6.4 billion, $3.7 billion in share purchases during the quarter, and $1.3 billion in dividends.
  • $16.4 billion in cash and equivalents on the balance sheet, with total debt of $21.2 billion.
  • Outlook for FY2026 with low double-digit growth expected for both revenue and, cost and EPS.

As can be seen above, this is a company in stellar financial health.

Visa has a recurrent, and much appreciated, habit of consistently beating estimates, which can appear small, but over time compound to the benefit of long-term shareholders. Beating revenue expectations with a few hundred million of dollars doesn’t seem like much, but if you do that consistently over many quarters, the upwards lift in forward expectations can be counted in the billions, as evident in the illustration.

We haven’t arrived at the valuation discussion yet, but it’s of course interesting to see how the company has continued the same financial trends for years but being valued at a lower multiple than earlier. Visa is forecasted to continued growing at low double digit in terms of revenue for the coming couple of years (beyond that, the time horizon becomes guess work). Point is, this management has proven it can meet its own guidance and even exceed it again and again.

With a payout ratio below 20% when measured on a free cash flow basis, it allows management to consistently buy back shares, suggesting that EPS will grow faster than revenue. Over the past decade, the company has reduced the number of outstanding shares by 18.6%.

I’ve extensively covered the pillar upon which the business foundation of Visa lies in my prior articles, but basically, this is a toll booth business that provides global networks and infrastructure to settle cross-border payments, digital & card payments.

The illustration above lists share of payments conducted in cash across countries throughout the world. A number of interesting observations can be made. First, that it’s feasible to get to below 10% of payments conducted in cash, under the assumption that payment infrastructure can handle it. South Korea, Norway, China, Iceland lead the way, all with less than 10% of payments completed via cash. Especially China sticks out here, a low-income developing country until “recently”, and still it’s leading within this space. That gives an indication of how swiftly a country can transition, if the culture and payment structure allows. On the other end we have developed countries such as Italy and Spain who still complete between 55-70% of their transactions via cash. A surprisingly high number, indicating resistance towards adapting cashless transactions. This suggests that some countries will make the transition faster, others slower. However, as it’s undeniable easier for the consumer, I’ll make the bold statement that eventually most countries will get to low double digit percentages, or potentially completely eradicate cash. There are many advantageous of doing so.

  • Shop owners who don’t carry cash are less exposed to robbery and handling fees
  • The black-market economy thrives on cash transactions, society at large has an interest in being able to follow and document payment flows for e.g. taxation
  • For the average joe, it’s less hassle to pay via card / phone compared to always having to scout for the nearest ATM.

Yes, there’ll always be that one person who resists adopting new habits, but eventually banks will offer less ATMs, branches and shop owners will refuse accepting cash payments as they come with a handling fee, at least my own country serves that example (one of the countries below 15%.

Coming back to the illustration, there are plenty of rich nations that have lots of room to grow their cashless transaction base, such as France, Ireland, Belgium, Portugal, Greece, Spain, Italy, all of which are countries where a minimum of 40% of payments are done via cash.

If we list developing countries, the list becomes even longer.

The point is, that Visa is addressing a market where growth is forecasted to continue for many years, perhaps decades, and while we have to accept that Visa won’t enjoy the duopoly/oligopoly market structure in all newer markets due to local competition, they will have a foothold in even those markets as the middle class grows and starts travelling, making use of the international payment structure.

Risks To My Thesis

Short-term, the biggest risk is the ongoing market volatility. President Trump has delivered a couple of blows to the market in form of the 2025 tariffs, and the ongoing war in Iran, which short-term can result in a recessionary environment, where Visa with its high beta value and above market valuation would be impacted hard. This is also one of the reasons why the company is currently trading at a discount. I’m European, and therefore follow European news outlets, and Visa together with Mastercard (MA) is being debated as two companies upon which Europe can’t accept having no alternative. What happens if suddenly the switch is turned off, and the network restructured, if the current or future administration grows tired of EU/US relations to the extent where it could become leverage in a negotiation. That seems to be a risk European politicians aren’t willing to accept, which somewhat impacts long-term expectations for the company amongst analysts. I’m not so sure personally as these topics go in and out of fashion, and before any action is taken, a new president will have taken office and perhaps it will all fizzle out into nothing. Additionally, I see no real alternative in EU as duplicating the billions of dollars poured into establishing the system aren’t just lying around, not to say anything about the lack of competence and legislation.

In a business context, the prime risk is connected to regulatory action. Visa is always under scrutiny due to the nature of its marketplace, as has been the example with several bills discussed in the US senate. The company is carrying incredibly high margins, so there is little doubt there is some kind of logic in considering how regulatory action can improve the situation for both B2C and B2B customers in the Visa and Mastercard infrastructure, where negotiation power is very unevenly distributed. As such, over time, I expect there will be some kind of regulatory action that would either break the consistent growth curve or carve out a chunk of company margins as pricing structures are changed. That prospect has always existed for these companies, and it didn’t scare me in 2016, and it doesn’t scare me a decade later either, but it could naturally impact Visa’s ability to grow consistently, which increases importance of the valuation at which one goes long the company.

The Best Valuation In Years

We already uncovered that Visa has consistently grown at double digits and is expected to continue doing so for the years to come. Risks appear to have increased in the short-term, which can explain some of the drawback, but so far, there is no difference to the underlying business performance.

Despite this, the company valuation is attractive outside the norm.

Visa’s valuation comes in as follows:

  • Price / Earning forward of 23.3
  • Price / Earning forward 1Y of 20.6
  • Price / Earning 5Y average of 34.2
  • Price / Earning 5Y mean of 34.9

The numbers above are fairly telling, at least when measured on a P/E multiple, Visa appears quite interesting.

  • Price / Sales forward of 12.9
  • Price / Sales forward 1Y of 11.6
  • Price / Sales 5Y average of 17.4

To put this into perspective, if Visa grows as forecasted, the company will be trading at a P/E of 18.5 by 2028.
